transcript.text: Great, how are you?
Not bad.
How do I pronounce your name?
Sorry.
It's Aida.
Aida, okay.
Nice to meet you.
I'm Ashish.
Nice to meet you as well.
Thanks for taking the time today to talk to us.
Yeah.
A quick intro.
I don't know if Robert has told you anything about us, but I'm a managing editor at GrowthX.
We're a content agency.
We use AI for a lot of our workflows.
We have an in-house platform that we use for content generation.
And the thing about AI is that it needs a lot of context, a lot of input.
Yep.
So we wanted to talk to you today to get some more insights on engineers specifically.
Okay.

Because I know Chris mentioned this early on in our engagement was that we have more information about architects and so far more content on website that talks to architects, but engineers are kind of a new content lane, so to say.
Right, right, right, right.
Fair enough.
I've prepared some questions for you today, but feel free to go off script.
If you feel like there's something that we need to say that's super important that I may have missed, feel free to let me know.
I would love to get started with just understanding engineers and project management and how that differs from architects.
Yeah, yeah.
And I mean, I should state that I'm not like an expert here.
You know, I think we're all kind of learning as we go.
But I think the things that we've learned so far have been that, generally speaking, engineers tend to work for kind of a subset of these larger projects that architects work in.
And so what that tends to mean is that they have shorter time periods than architects.
They might, they probably have a lot more concurrent projects than architects.
So they're managing like much higher volume.
So
Again, this is like, on average, I'm sure there are exceptions to all these rules, but they spend a lot more time on, I guess, yeah, they're kind of working with a lot of concurrent projects.
I think what that also means in terms of like our software is some of the things related to planning and kind of long-term stuff is a little bit less useful.
And the stuff that's more useful is things that we can automate and kind of do bulk actions and things like that.

So, you know, that's where, you know, similarly, we're using AI to kind of speed up various processes around like creating invoices and other kinds of areas where we can pull in contracts and create projects for them and things like that.
Because the volume at which engineers are doing things definitely is much higher than what architects do.
Mm-hmm.
Yeah.
So the engineer...
What that you work with, are they usually a part of engineering firms, or are they part of cross-functional teams?
Oh, yeah, good question.
Most of our folks, the whole firm is engineering.
Okay.
And so they might work with an architect, but generally speaking, that architect is not part of the same firm, and so it's kind of like a firm collaboration.
Mm-hmm.
Okay.
Who do you, like, who would you say, like, among the, like, if we were talking about a broad, like, engineering persona, who is the primary one?
Like, is it someone who's more of, like, a principal or an owner of an engineering firm, or is it someone that's, like, more in the weeds in the project day-to-day work?
When, like, for what, like, who we should be talking to, or?
Yeah, yeah.
So, like, content, yeah, I'm, like, thinking about, like, who's the one that's making the, like, who's the buying committee, who influences?
Yeah, yeah.
I think generally speaking, I mean, this is probably true for both architects and engineers, is I think generally, like, the principal or the kind of owner tends to be the main decision maker.
And if any, and they might pull in some project managers.
I mean, you know, I think it's a little bit different in different orgs.
But the other person that tends to have a lot of say is kind of whoever's running the finances.
So that could be anyone from, like, a bookkeeper to, like, a proper CFO in some orgs because, obviously, because they control whether you spend money on this.
But more importantly, you know, a lot of the financial workflows, the invoicing, the payments, and all that kind of stuff tends to be the most complicated work in these firms.
And so having buy-in from that person is pretty important.
Mm-hmm.
Yeah.
This might be a very, like, simple or a very broad question.
But what...
Why are the invoices in the billing, why is that such a complicated process for engineering firms?
Yeah, and I think it's true for both architects and engineers.

Like, so, well, number one, it doesn't have to be if they use our software, but I think generally speaking, what happens is like in these service businesses where you're figuring out how many people, how much time people have spent and you might be charging by the hour.
So there's a lot to figure out in terms of that for kind of phases or projects where you're charging that way.
And then in cases where you're not necessarily charging that way, but you still want to see kind of progress.
think generally speaking in a services-based industry, you still want to know how much time you're spending on various things and whether that's profitable and things like that.
So that's like in figuring out kind of what the number should be.
And then there's also just the nature of these projects.
Again, like I think with engineers sometimes...
It's not as much as architects, but they tend to have lots of phases, so like different parts of the project that they might be working on or towards.
And so making it really clear for the end client, so oftentimes maybe for an engineer, the end client's an architect, or a developer or something like that.

Like just making it super clear what is exactly being charged for and why, potentially having kind of audit logs or trail logs of like, this is how much time various people spent on these activities, that kind of thing.
So it can be pretty onerous.
And then also if you're using kind of generic tools like QuickBooks or something like that to create this stuff, they're not really built for any of that.
And so you end up doing a lot of kind of manual manipulation or create, you know, some people do the extreme of creating invoices just in like a.
You know, a design tool or something.
So then it has no linkage back to anything else and doesn't support payments and things like that.
So, again, I mean, it can be really easy if you follow the method that we use.
But historically, I think people have made it more complicated than it needs to be.
Do you find it easy to get engineers to buy into the method that you're promoting?
Or do they take a little more time to convince that, like, the way they're doing things right now just doesn't work?
Yeah, I think there's, like, two flavors of people.
I think there's, I think the ones that come to us generally feel like, you know, there are things that they could be doing better.
And so, therefore, I think they're more amenable.

And then I think, on average, the smaller the firm, the less, and maybe the less established, like, from, like, a time period, the more willing they are to kind of change how they do things, for sure.
sure.
So yeah, there's that.
And then there's others that need convincing.
And I think the challenge with Monograph in general is that, I mean, if you are willing to make these changes, like a lot of things can be easier.

But because we cover kind of everything, project management, invoicing, staffing, all this stuff, it might require, or at least requires maybe not the right word, but you'll benefit the most if you change a lot of these things.
And sometimes someone might come in, because they're really interested in one aspect of that, like, oh, I really like your project management, or I really like staffing, or voicing, whatever.
And then there may be less, either they don't have the influence on the other part of the workflows, or they're just less willing to like change everything up.
So I don't know, I'm going broad, because hopefully you're, I can figure this out.
But like, you know, just, yeah, so I think there's a mix, for sure.
I do think the people that are most successful and get the most gains from Monograph are the...
The ones that are most willing to kind of modernize or change how they're working to kind of optimize for Monograph.
Absolutely.
Are there any specific engineering disciplines that struggle more with their current project management setup than others?
You know, that's a good question that I probably am not the best to answer.
I think if you probably talk to someone in the sales board that's been talking more closely with them, they've probably identified that.
I don't think I have enough, like, knowledge to answer that.
Yeah, sure.
When it comes to, like, platform features, do you know if there are any features that, you know, tend to prefer more than architects?
Or is it kind of more or less the same?
I think from what I've seen so far, it's more or less
It's the same.
It's just that, as I was mentioning earlier, I think the engineers are more, on average, again, are more interested in things that kind of can be done in, like, at high volumes quickly.
So batch, you know, actions of various kinds, because, again, they tend to be doing lots of projects at the same time.
And so anything that's good at that, they care a bit more about, whereas, again, on average, an architect is maybe doing a little bit less of that.
And so they might care a little bit more about the customization or the look and feel and things like that of certain things versus, like, kind of high volume activity.
Right, right.
Okay.
Do you know, I mean, this is probably something you might not know off the top of your head.
Are there any really good success stories with engineering firms that you can think of?
I don't know off the top of my head, but there are definitely some.
think actually Chris would be a good person, too.
I think he's collected a bunch of testimonials.
Okay.
Perfect.
I know you were already kind of mentioning different operational problems that, you know, engineering firm owners struggle with.
What would you say would be like the top three that like really keep them up, keep them up at night?
Yeah.
Okay.

So I think cash flow is always, you know, number one for any small business and especially for these, you know, they are often in most cases, not every single time, but in most cases, they're kind of two steps away from the payer, right?
So there might be a developer, let's say, working with an architect, and then the architect is consulting out to this engineer.
And so oftentimes the way their contracts work is Okay.

They'll get paid once the developer pays the architect, and so they don't actually have a ton of control around, you know, like, they don't have direct contact with that developer, so they don't know.
so oftentimes the kind of the delay between the work that they did and the actual time that they get paid could be quite large and long.
And so I think getting paid quickly and at flow is a big challenge.

Then I think, yeah, probably, like, managing, so staffing, I'd say, like, because they have lots and lots of projects on average, more so than architects, there's a lot to kind of shuffle and move people around, and they might be doing that on a very regular basis every week, every, maybe even in a shorter time period than that, so they have to move people around.
And then, let's see, what would number three be?
I'm guessing that it would...
It'd be like, I mean, so I think there's the staffing element and then there's the other kind of project management stuff.
So keeping track of the status of various projects, maybe having to pause and restart them, coupling people around.
You know, it's related to the staffing for sure, but I think there are other aspects of project management that they generally are going on site more.
So there, you know, there's a lot more kind of managing the logistics around that.
So I think all of that kind of encompasses that project management challenge.
Yeah, yeah.
You mentioned how a lot of times engineers, they kind of partner up with architects and support their projects.
Do you have any insights into like what kind of issues engineers run into when working with architects aside from like the delays and payments?
Yes.
Yeah.
So delays in payments are number one.
I think just general communication around timelines.

So it might not be the payment per se, but, you know, all of the, like, you know, oftentimes either because the end owner, developer, whoever has made some kind of changes or the architect has, you know, their timelines might be changing.
A deliverable might be due later or earlier than, you know, originally intended.
And the way they communicate can, you know, is probably just over email and, you know, they're not in regular and fast communication.
And so that, that's probably like the number one challenge.
And of course that also encapsulates the payment side of things.

Yeah, I think, you know, related could be like scope changes or things of that nature again, because they are not the direct, in most cases, they're not directly working with the end, you know, user that they tend to have.
But they're, they're just kind of one step.
Removed from a lot of the communication around scope, timeline, budget, et cetera.
Okay, great, great.
When you're working, like you mentioned that, you know, some of the engineering firms that you start working with, if they're small, they have a very basic setup.
What about engineering firms that come to you and they're already working with some type of project management software like BQE?
Are there any differences in their approach, what they're looking for?
What are they frustrated with?
What's not working?
Yeah, yeah.
So I think generally speaking, when they're using other tools in the space, those tools were built.
They're really accounting tools that were built in like the 80s and 90s.
And they, you know, there's nothing wrong with that, but they're not necessarily built for usability.
And they're not built for kind of what the expectations of their workers are today.

And so I think typically there's kind of a myriad of frustrations, but number one is like in order to, you know, run a services business, you have to have a very accurate idea of like where people are spending their time.
And a lot of these other tools, just something as simple as the time tracking is very cumbersome.
And so, and whenever, you know, something is unpleasant, people don't want to do it.
And so that they'll eventually, you know, record their time, but they'll do it later.
And so it will be inaccurate or it will be out of date or what have you.
And so that like it all starts from there, like that core amount of data is already generally wrong.
Then, you know, other aspects, things like invoicing and areas like that, they've built these like very flexible tools, which like might sound good, but we actually at Monograph feel the opposite.
Like we want, we're purposely fairly opinionated on how we want things to work and not just because we think.

You know, we're the experts or something like that, but rather because we know that if we pick a workflow and everyone follows it, we can do a lot around automation, simplification, all the different pieces fitting together.
And that stuff is just not the case for these other types of tools.
And so the frustrations kind of lie all across the board.
The data is inaccurate in the first place.
Then, you know, if you do the wrong thing, you can mess it up even further.
There's just, there's almost like infinite possibility for you to kind of screw things up.
And what tends to happen in those situations is you do, or you have somebody spending a ton of energy just to make these tools work.
You might have a full-time person just like running this tool.
And that is just not the way that, you know, Monograph works.
And so to come back to your question, like when they come to us, generally speaking, they're really frustrated with like, even if theoretically these tools have, you know.
X, Y, Z feature and all these different things.
In general, they're very hard to use.
They don't actually use a lot of those features because they don't work as well as they should or you need to be an expert in order to get it to all work.
And they're excited to come to Monograph because, of course, there is a learning curve to potentially change how you've done things, et cetera.
But once you do that, I think they see the power and a lot of this gets automated away.
You don't even have to do a lot of the things that you were doing with these other tools because we just kind of take care of a lot of that for you.
The data is always accurate because people are actually doing the things like tracking their time.
And yeah, and then everything can kind of work from that.
Yeah, that's perfect.
I mean, it reminds me a lot about Airtable, for example.
We use it internally and it's a great tool, but it's so flexible to a point where you're kind of
You get paralysis just coming into it.
So I totally get what you're talking about.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And, you know, those tools can be powerful and, you know, with the right guardrails around them, like maybe they could work well.
Like that does require probably someone internally that's helping you guys, like, figure out, okay, here, what you can do, here's what you can't do and all that.
And, you know, that's a lot of work for somebody potentially, right?
Mm-hmm.
Mm-hmm.
Yeah, absolutely.

Um, this question might be a little bit, like, um, it's very content related, um, but I would love to get your thoughts on, like, if you could write, like, three or four blogs for Monograph, um, and, you know, these blogs have to convince engineering firms to switch to Monograph, what kind of topics would you cover?
Oh, uh, good question.
I mean, so I do, like I
But I think cash flow is king.
So I think like really, now I think the trick is, I'm actually not as sure with engineers.
I know with architects, what motivates them is actually not the profitability of their firm or, you know, in general, again, generalizing here.
But most of them are in it for the passion of the work.
And then, you know, if they make money, they're happy, but that's not really the kind of mean motivator.
That being said, I think it's their biggest challenge.
And so I do think a blog post that talks a lot about, you know, how to be effective, how to, you know, ensure that you're, you know, making your payroll and things like that, I think will resonate.
And so everything from, you know, Robert talks about it, but like, make sure you're invoicing every month, make sure you're using electronic payment.
And make it like really, the friction is really low.
Make sure you're kind of really clear about your expectations.
Make sure you have your profits set up.
Make sure you're learning each time and like getting better at your prices.
And like those kinds of pieces, I think would resonate with people.
It's like these are, maybe do it in a way that doesn't feel overwhelming.
Because I think, again, I don't know, I think I have a sense that engineers maybe are a little bit more business minded.
Whereas I think with architects, I think a lot of them can get scared if it sounds too complicated or, you know, like you get too jargony.
But my instinct as engineers, that might resonate with them more.
I'm like a mix.
So that would be one.
The second, I'm like a mixed, or like, I'm a little ambivalent here, but I think something.

Working around automation and AI could work, obviously it's done, so the reason I'm mixed about it is because it's done to death, everyone talks about it, it's like, okay, blah, blah, blah, you know, so I think it would have to be done in an authentic way, but, you know, giving some insight to them about how, you know, you can use it maybe more, I think you guys maybe actually posted a blog post like similar to this, I think I saw yesterday, but, you know, like, just generally how you could use it in all of your workflows, including, you know, with monographs and projects, I think makes sense.
And then, you know, I think the other area is like how, so we, we haven't really like published this yet, but we've been trying to get more, more clear about our
Or opinionated workflows.
We've kind of referred to it internally as the Monograph Method.
So these are the ways that if you run your business this way and you use Monograph, like, things will work better for you.
Trying to think if that would resonate with engineers or not.
Like, I think what I've seen is engineers might be, it might take them a little bit longer to get convinced to change their ways for the sake of all of this.
But I think they've done in the right way.

And so maybe it's not too centered on Monograph, but it's like, here are kind of best practices that, you know, like, whether it's around staffing, whether it's around how you manage lots of projects, how you get, how you communicate with architects better, like all of those kind of pain points that I mentioned earlier.
Maybe there's kind of just like sharing some best practices.
Maybe it's from, it's advice from some of our other firms, right?
I think that's maybe the other thing that you asked about success stories.
I think that stuff resonates better than Monograph kind of telling you how we think it should be.
Rather, let our customers or let our successful clients kind of talk about how they've done it and hopefully kind of peppering in how they use Monograph.
But more importantly, it's coming from a credible source versus us.
Yeah, yeah, I definitely agree.
I know you have a wealth of case studies.
We've referenced, I think, one or two in a recent blog post.
All right, perfect.
I think I got a lot of great insights from you.
Is there anything else that you might want to mention that I missed?

No, other than I would say, I don't know who else you're talking to, but I definitely feel like some of the folks in the sales team that have been like a little bit closer to the engineers, I think we'll be able to give you.
A little bit more specifics, I think I was fairly general in what my hypothesis is, but I think they might be able to kind of either validate that or give you more specifics.
So I would suggest doing that if you're not already.
All right, perfect.
Yeah, we'll definitely reach out, try to set some time up.
Sales themes are generally very difficult to reach.
Yeah, yeah, I would go through Chris and he could probably hit you up with the right person.
We have what we call a tiger team, very focused on the engineering group.
So I would maybe get someone from that group that could help.
And yeah, would probably ask Chris to kind of help set that up.
Because like you said, it's fun to get it.
Yeah.
Okay.
All right, perfect.
Well, thank you so much for your time.
It was great talking.
you.
And yeah, I'll be, I might follow up with a quick request, but that'll be a little later.
I'll provide more details in Slack.
So.
Okay.
All right.
Thanks.
Have a great day.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Gather insights on engineering firms and their project management needs for content creation at GrowthX.](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Engineers typically manage higher volumes of shorter, concurrent projects compared to architects](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=91.0)
  - [Cash flow, staffing, and project management are top operational challenges for engineering firms](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=691.0)
  - [Monograph's opinionated, automation-focused approach addresses pain points of traditional PM tools](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=1106.0)

## Topics

### Engineer vs Architect Project Management

  - [Engineers work on subsets of larger projects, with shorter timelines and higher concurrency](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=91.0)
  - [Focus on automating bulk actions and speeding up processes (e.g., invoicing, contract management)](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=148.0)
  - [Less emphasis on long-term planning tools compared to architects](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=134.0)

### Engineering Firm Structure and Decision Making

  - [Most Monograph clients are pure engineering firms, collaborating with separate architectural firms](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=190.0)
  - [Key decision-makers: firm principals/owners, project managers, and finance leads (e.g., CFOs, bookkeepers)](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=242.0)
  - [Financial workflows (invoicing, payments) are often the most complex, requiring buy-in from finance personnel](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=280.0)

### Invoicing and Billing Challenges

  - [Service-based billing (hourly rates, time tracking) adds complexity](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=312.0)
  - [Multiple project phases and the need for clear client communication complicate invoicing](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=369.0)
  - [Generic tools like QuickBooks aren't optimized for these workflows, leading to manual processes](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=400.0)

### Monograph Adoption and Success Factors

  - [Smaller, less established firms tend to be more adaptable to Monograph's methods](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=465.0)
  - [Full benefits realized when firms are willing to change multiple workflows (PM, invoicing, staffing)](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=491.0)
  - [High-volume, quick-action features are particularly valued by engineering firms](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=601.0)

### Top Operational Challenges for Engineering Firms

1.  Cash flow management (often due to being "two steps away" from the payer)
2.  Staffing and resource allocation across numerous concurrent projects
3.  Project management logistics (status tracking, on-site work coordination)

### Engineer-Architect Collaboration Issues

  - [Communication challenges around timeline changes and scope adjustments](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=840.0)
  - [Limited direct contact with end clients/developers can complicate project management](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=898.0)

### Monograph vs Traditional PM Software

  - [Legacy tools often lack usability and modern workflow optimization](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=943.0)
  - [Monograph's opinionated approach enables better automation and data accuracy](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=1014.0)
  - [Reduced need for dedicated tool management personnel](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=1062.0)

### Content Strategy for Engineering Firms

1.  Cash flow optimization strategies (invoicing frequency, electronic payments, pricing)
2.  Authentic exploration of automation and AI applications in engineering workflows
3.  Best practices and success stories from peer firms using Monograph

## Next Steps

  - [Reach out to Chris to connect with Monograph's "tiger team" focused on engineering clients](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=1528.0)
  - [Consider interviewing sales team members for more specific insights on engineering firms](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=1528.0)
  - [Develop content strategy based on suggested topics, emphasizing peer success stories](https://fathom.video/share/R6sxkzrzyUoYQyiUT1UxDTEnxhJ7vkp2?tab=summary&timestamp=1549.0)

action_items.description: Reach out to Chris to set up meeting with Monograph's engineering-focused "tiger team" sales member
